source_statement_text: In supraglottic cT3 lesions, transoral laser microsurgery, and transoral robotic surgery are viable strategies in the case of limited invasion of the preepiglottic space (ie, not abutting the thyrohyoid membrane). Otherwise, type I (in the case of more pronounced pre-epiglottic space involvement) or IIB open partial horizontal laryngectomy (in patients with thyroid cartilage erosion) or non-surgical organ preservation protocols should be considered.

source_statement_text: Performance status, comorbidities, and tumour-node-metastasis stage have prognostic significance for overall survival in laryngeal and hypopharyngeal cancer treated with larynx-preservation strategies.

source_statement_text: Tumour stage, volume of primary and nodal disease, comorbidities, hypopharyngeal origin, and tumour metabolic activity have been associated with progression-free survival and laryngeal preservation.

normalized_value_transitions: pre_epiglottic_space: limited -> none, primary_site: supraglottic -> hypopharyngeal

source_statement_text: Having an impaired pharyngolaryngeal functional status (tumour-related dysphagia requiring feeding tube, or recurring pneumonia within 12 months requiring hospitalisation) or a tracheostomy before non-surgical larynx-preservation treatments are associated with poor functional outcomes.

source_statement_text: Response to induction chemotherapy (complete or partial response) allows for the appropriate selection of patients who might benefit from non-surgical laryngeal preservation strategy. The evaluation of response to induction chemotherapy should take into account both clinical assessment (ie, symptoms and endoscopic findings) and morphological imaging (ie, contrast-enhanced CT, MRI, or both). Metabolic imaging (ie, PET–CT) might be useful to complete the evaluation, if feasible. A radiological response of at least 50% is considered a favourable prognostic factor and a predictor of response for non-surgical larynx-preservation treatment.
benchmark_item_labels: PR ≥50% — shifts from RT alone to CRT
normalized_value_transitions: ict_response: complete_response -> partial_response_50plus, vc_remobilization_post_ict: true -> false, vocal_cord_mobility: normal -> partially_impaired

source_statement_text: In the case of vocal cord fixation, larynx remobilisation is associated with functional larynx preservation after definitive radiotherapy in organ-preservation protocols that include induction chemotherapy.
benchmark_item_labels: PR ≥50% — shifts from RT alone to CRT, VC remobilization (improves prognosis but doesn't change eligibility)
normalized_value_transitions: ict_response: complete_response -> partial_response_50plus, vc_remobilization_post_ict: false -> true, vc_remobilization_post_ict: true -> false, vocal_cord_mobility: fixed -> partially_impaired, vocal_cord_mobility: normal -> partially_impaired

source_statement_text: Transoral laser microsurgery is not indicated as a first-line treatment in locally advanced hypopharyngeal cancer.
benchmark_item_labels: Site → glottic (SITE CROSSOVER), Site → hypopharyngeal, Site → hypopharyngeal (SITE-CONDITIONAL)

benchmark_item_labels: PR ≥50% — shifts from RT alone to CRT

source_statement_text: Subsequent curative treatment with radiotherapy and concomitant platinum-based chemotherapy is indicated for patients with stable disease after induction chemotherapy who refuse total laryngectomy.

section: Listening to the patient’s preferences: tools and implementation
source_statement_text: In cases of advanced disease (such as cT4a tumours with high-volume extralaryngeal extension) or no response to induction chemotherapy, the treating multidisciplinary team should be clear that larynx-preservation strategies have a reduced functional and survival outcome compared with total laryngectomy. Patients should undergo extensive counselling to be able to fully grasp the potential detrimental consequences of tentatively favouring quality over quantity of life when making a decision concerning treatment.
benchmark_item_labels: Progression → TL clearly indicated
normalized_value_transitions: ict_response: complete_response -> progression, vc_remobilization_post_ict: true -> false, vocal_cord_mobility: normal -> fixed

source_statement_text: The identification of suitable candidates for open partial horizontal laryngectomy should consider the baseline clinical factors and physiological stress that follows the surgical procedure. Good cardiopulmonary and neurological functions and a good performance status are necessary prerequisites.

source_statement_text: Non-surgical organ preservation treatments are contraindicated in patients with severe pretreatment laryngo-oesophageal dysfunction, which is clinically defined by the following elements: laryngeal penetration or aspiration, or recurrent pneumonia. Relative contraindications are pretreatment tracheostomy or pretreatment feeding tube, particularly regarding the recovery of laryngeal and oesophageal function.

Non-surgical preservation can be considered only in patients who decline total laryngectomy provided that they are extensively informed about the inferior oncological outcome and the higher risk of toxic effects.

source_statement_text: Overall, total laryngectomy with adjuvant treatment (radiotherapy with or without chemotherapy) confers longer survival, better functional outcomes, and better quality of life than non-surgical treatment in patients affected by unselected cT4a laryngeal SCC (LoE: III). However, equal outcomes are achievable through organ-preserving treatments in carefully selected patients with preserved laryngeal function and minimal anterior extra-laryngeal extension.

section: Which comorbidities are contraindications and to what extent?
source_statement_text: Patients unfit for chemotherapy are not suitable candidates for induction chemotherapy-based or chemoradiation-based approaches for laryngeal preservation.

normalized_value_transitions: comorbidity_burden: none_mild -> severe

section: Which comorbidities are contraindications and to what extent?
source_statement_text: Absolute contraindications to high-dose cisplatin-based chemotherapy (administered as induction chemotherapy or concurrent treatment) include: any comorbidity that independently results in an ECOG performance status of 2 or more, renal failure (defined as a creatinine clearance <50 mL/min), grade ≥2 neurological disorders (diabetic neuropathy, peripheral sensory neuropathy, orthostatic hypotension, Lhermitte’s sign, seizures, and focal encephalopathy), pregnancy or lactation, child-Pugh B or C liver disease, grade >2 bone marrow disorders (haemoglobin level under 8 g/dL; neutrophil count <1000 cells per µL or platelet count < 100 000 cells per µL), congestive heart failure class III or IV according to the New York Heart Association despite cardiovascular optimisation therapy, congestive heart failure NYHA class I or II with left ventricular ejection fraction equal to or under 50%, uncontrolled chronic viral HBV or HCV infection without antiviral treatment, HIV infection with a CD4 cell count <200 cells per µL, detectable viral load, and AIDS, hypersensitivity to platinum-based therapy, severe psychiatric disorders interfering with treatment compliance, and uncontrolled insulin-dependent diabetes.

section: Which comorbidities are contraindications and to what extent?
source_statement_text: Relative contraindications to high-dose cisplatin-based chemotherapy (administered as induction chemotherapy or concurrent treatment) include: reatinine clearance of 50–60 mL/min, grade >1 hearing or neurological disorders, grade 2 bone marrow, liver, and lung disorders, previous heart disease, diabetes, recurrent pulmonary infections, concomitant use of nephrotoxic drugs, HIV infection with CD4 cell count 200–350 cells per µL, and impaired social and economic support.

section: Which comorbidities are contraindications and to what extent?
source_statement_text: For patients unfit for any systemic treatment who decline total laryngectomy , hyperfractionated or accelerated radiotherapy alone could be offered. Due to the expected lower oncological outcome compared with the two validated chemoradiation approaches, careful follow-up is suggested to avoid delay in salvage surgery.
benchmark_item_labels: Severe comorbidity burden → unfit for chemo (S67)

section: Which comorbidities are contraindications and to what extent?
source_statement_text: Gene repair disorders (ie, xeroderma pigmentosum, ataxia-telangiectasia, and Fanconi anaemia) preclude a non-surgical larynx-preservation approach; previous radiotherapy to the neck is a relative contraindication depending on previous doses, volumes, and time elapsed from treatment.
benchmark_item_labels: DNA repair disorder → absolute CI non-surgical LP (S74R), Previous neck RT → relative CI non-surgical LP (S74R)
normalized_value_transitions: dna_repair_disorder: false -> true, previous_neck_rt: false -> true

section: Which comorbidities are contraindications and to what extent?
source_statement_text: Neurodegenerative and neuromuscular diseases potentially affecting swallowing and respiratory functions might be a contraindication to larynx preservation treatments (surgical and non-surgical).

section: Which comorbidities are contraindications and to what extent?
source_statement_text: In patients with moderate comorbidities and an ECOG performance status of 2, it is crucial to distinguish whether the performace status is the result of tumour progression or the patient’s comorbidities. This differentiation is essential to determine the appropriate indication for cisplatin in laryngeal preservation. Specifically, if the performance scale of 2 is influenced by the disease itself, cisplatin could be indicated if there are no absolute or relative contraindications to cisplatin-based chemotherapy; and if the performance scale of 2 is influenced by comorbidities or toxicity due to previous treatments, cisplatin is contraindicated.
benchmark_item_labels: ECOG 2 comorbidity-driven → cisplatin ABSOLUTE CI (SOURCE-CONDITIONAL), ECOG 2 tumor-driven → cisplatin STILL OK (SOURCE-CONDITIONAL)
normalized_value_transitions: cardiopulmonary_function: good -> impaired, ecog_ps: 0 -> 2_comorbidity_related, ecog_ps: 0 -> 2_tumor_related, ecog_ps_source: null -> comorbidity_driven, ecog_ps_source: null -> tumor_driven

source_statement_text: The following extensions are absolute contraindications for transoral laser microsurgery in T3 laryngeal cancer: posterior laryngeal involvement requiring bilateral arytenoid resection (LoE: V); inner cortical infiltration (even if focal) of the thyroid cartilage (LoE: IV); posterior paraglottic involvement (LoE: IV); anterior commissure involvement with difficult exposure (LoE: IV); extensive supraglottic tumours with massive infiltration of the pre-epiglottic space (LoE: V); and subglottic extension under 10 mm in the posterior part of the glottis (LoE: IV).
benchmark_item_labels: AC difficult exposure → TLM blocked, AC difficult exposure → TLM relative CI, Focal TC inner cortex (TREATMENT-CONDITIONAL), Massive PES abutting membrane → TLM blocked, Posterior paraglottic space → TLM blocked, Posterior subglottic <10mm → TLM blocked

section: Organ preservation in older patients: selection criteria
source_statement_text: Age itself is not a contraindication to surgical and non-surgical laryngeal preservation approaches. However, the common comorbidities and frailty associated with age might have a great effect on both treatment choice and outcome. Fit older patients (≥70 years) should be offered the same larynx-preservation strategies as their younger counterparts.

section: Organ preservation in older patients: selection criteria
source_statement_text: Vulnerable and frail patients are at higher risk of treatment complications and face a higher risk of non-cancer-related death, due to comorbidities and treatment-induced toxic effects. Therefore, organ preservation strategies in this group of patients should be carefully weighed against the risks.
benchmark_item_labels: Frail → OPHL relative CI, adapted approach
normalized_value_transitions: frailty_status: fit -> frail, g8_score: above_14 -> below_14

section: Organ preservation in older patients: selection criteria
source_statement_text: In patients aged ≥70 years, frailty (defined by the Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment) is a relative contraindication to open partial horizontal laryngectomy, even in the absence of severe neurological or cardio-respiratory comorbidities.
